behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels for downstream attribution
behaviors.justification: Speaker labels come from the real external speech-to-text API. A mock API could return any labels; only calling the real service reveals whether it actually supports speaker labels, formats them correctly, and handles multi-speaker audio. Mocking this dependency always passes, hiding real API failures or format incompatibilities.
behaviors.test_description: Send a multi-speaker audio file (2+ speakers) to the actual speech-to-text service. Verify the transcript includes speaker labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1:', 'Speaker 2:') with correct identifiers and coherent turn-taking. Fails if the real API doesn't provide labels, returns inconsistent formats, or loses speaker information for certain audio codecs.

behaviors.description: Structured summary includes at least one action item for each attendee assigned a task
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on the real LLM's ability to parse the transcript, recognize task assignments (explicit and implicit), and attribute them correctly to attendees. A mocked LLM could always return the expected structure; only a real LLM call reveals whether it misses assignments, conflates attendees, or fails to recognize task verbs.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a transcript where 3+ attendees are explicitly assigned different tasks ('Alice will write the spec', 'Bob will review', 'Carol will deploy'). Call the real LLM summarization endpoint. Verify each attendee appears in the action items section with at least one action attributed to them and the attributions are accurate. A mock would always pass; a real LLM might merge attendees, miss implicit assignments, or misparse task syntax.

behaviors.description: Key decisions from transcript appear in 'decisions' section, not buried in narrative
behaviors.justification: The LLM must reason about which statements are decisions, extract them, and place them in the structured output field. This is pure LLM classification and structuring logic. A mocked LLM could always populate the decisions field correctly; only a real LLM call reveals whether it misclassifies narrative as decisions or fails to extract subtle decisions.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a transcript containing explicit decision statements ('We decided to migrate to Kubernetes', 'We agreed to use the vendor's API') interspersed with contextual narrative. Call the real LLM summarization. Verify decisions are populated in the decisions section and the narrative section does not duplicate them. Fails if the real LLM conflates narrative with decisions, misses decision verbs like 'agreed' or 'will adopt', or leaves decisions scattered in narrative.

behaviors.description: Audio longer than 60 minutes is segmented, transcribed separately, and merged into coherent summary
behaviors.justification: Requires orchestrated steps: (1) detect and segment long audio, (2) transcribe each segment via real external API, (3) merge transcripts and summaries with an LLM to ensure coherence. Mocking segmentation logic, transcription API, or merge logic could hide real failures. The failure boundary spans multiple services and agents; only end-to-end execution discovers integration issues.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real 90-minute meeting recording. Run the pipeline and verify: (a) the service segments it correctly (e.g., 45-min chunks), (b) each segment is transcribed via the real API with speaker labels preserved, (c) transcripts are merged with the LLM into a single coherent summary with no duplicate sections, consistent speaker references, and coherent topic flow. Unit tests for segmentation, mocked transcription, or mocked LLM merge would all pass even if the real workflow fails due to API incompatibilities, timeout handling, or merge logic bugs.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline (upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output) completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: Multiple agentic and I/O steps (audio upload, real external transcription API, real LLM summarization), orchestration across services, and error recovery logic. Each step could fail independently or in combination. Only end-to-end execution with real infrastructure reveals orchestration bugs, timeout handling gaps, format mismatches between services, or missing error recovery.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real meeting recording (or high-fidelity substitute) with 45+ minutes of multi-speaker discussion and mixed audio quality. Invoke the complete pipeline end-to-end. Verify: (a) audio is uploaded without manual intervention, (b) transcription completes with speaker labels, (c) summarization produces a structured output with all fields (transcript, decisions, action items, attendees), (d) no errors or manual prompts are surfaced, (e) the full process completes within a reasonable timeout. Unit and integration mocks would always pass; only the real pipeline reveals API timeouts, format incompatibilities, error handling gaps, or orchestration deadlocks.
